workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: NBA team beat reporting is an in-person deadline job built from repeated arena access, source conversations, observation, note discipline, and fast digital filing. Source material describes reporters attending shootaround and games, talking to coaches and players before and after games, transcribing and organizing interviews, watching injuries and rotations, and writing instant stories after the buzzer. The upgraded simulator therefore mixes physical arena observation, spoken availability, Slack/editor communication, source sorting, and deadline writing rather than treating the role as generic prose composition.

sourceEvidence: Beat reporters are physically at games, practices, shootarounds, media entrances, courtside areas, and postgame access spaces.
Courtside and beat reporting source material emphasizes notebook/recorder use, observing player movement, and working from the arena media table.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Include a playable arena observation scene where the student inspects visible court/media-row evidence and records a caveated observation for the later story.

sourceEvidence: Reporters work in Slack/editor channels, CMS drafts, live box scores, notes, transcripts, injury reports, and social rumor streams.
The same-day game article depends on turning digital and observed inputs into publishable copy under a clock.
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Use realistic document, Slack, kanban, and CMS-style surfaces with task-specific reference tabs.

sourceEvidence: The reporter must decide what is newsworthy, what is verified, what needs attribution, and what should be held back.
Sources emphasize separating observation from confirmed information and choosing a focused angle under deadline.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: Keep the lead-angle pivot, note classification, and editor recommendation as explicit judgment moments.

sourceEvidence: Beat reporters write recaps, follow-up angles, source notes, and deadline stories after interviews and games.
Source material repeatedly describes writing instant stories right after the buzzer and revising with editor direction.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require a reporting plan, observation note, note rationale, deadline article, editor update, and final story package.

sourceEvidence: The role involves asking coaches and players concise questions during media availability and scrums.
Beat reporting depends on source relationships, respectful follow-ups, and extracting usable attribution without leading.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Use in-person voice scenes for coach availability and the player scrum, with prep notes visible.

sourceEvidence: Game-night reporting includes waiting for media doors, watching warmups, monitoring a live game, and reacting to late updates.
The reporter works across pregame, live game, postgame access, and filing windows.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Represent waiting and monitoring through live game notes, app-specific updates, and deadline constraints, not passive click-through narration.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The active flow starts with a bounded newsroom angle choice, then has the student build a reporting plan from visible source material, perform an in-arena observation, ask coach and player questions in person, capture organized possession timeline notes, sort postgame notes, write a deadline article, update the editor, and file a final package. The physical scene is intentionally secondary but meaningful; the writing, source sorting, and communication scenes remain the dominant experience.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: Active scenes include one MCQ pivot, one structured reporting plan, one physical playground, one possession timeline, two in-person voice conversations, one note-classification board, two writing tasks, and one Slack editor update. That balance mirrors the source-derived job mix for an entry-level NBA beat reporter.

intro.title: Welcome to Full Court Wire
intro.content: You are an entry-level NBA beat reporter at Full Court Wire. Tonight you are covering the fictional Harbor City Cyclones from inside the arena: choose a verifiable angle, use the right materials at the right time, observe Reed in person, ask coach and player questions, sort notes, and file copy your editor can trust on deadline.
intro.imageBrief: First-person view from an NBA arena media-row work table before tipoff. A laptop shows a CMS draft, live box score, Slack editor thread, and credential badge for Harbor City Arena; a notebook and recorder sit beside the keyboard while the court glows in the background.

assignment_recap.title: 4:18 PM Editor Recap
assignment_recap.content: You are on media row before warmups with Tessa's recap open and your deadline already set. Before you chase a story, lock in the newsroom frame: what is known about Reed, why this matchup matters, and what still needs on-record confirmation.
assignment_recap.imageBrief: Arena media room laptop with a short editor Slack recap visible, a notebook, phone recorder, credential badge, and the court tunnel in the background. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the actual recap is rendered by the simulator UI.

memoryCard.title: Game-night frame
bullets: Your game article is due at 11:10 PM ET.
Malik Reed is returning from a right ankle sprain.
The question is whether Coach Harris can trust Reed in the final minutes.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to invent extra context here. Hold onto the editor frame and use the task tabs when you need details.

turns.coworkerLine: Malik Reed is a veteran guard returning from a right ankle sprain. The team lists him available tonight, and no minutes limit is listed.
answerFacts: Malik Reed is returning from a right ankle sprain.
The team lists Malik Reed as available tonight.
No minutes limit is listed for Malik Reed.

turns.coworkerLine: Harbor City's late-game lineups have been unsettled without Reed. Denver's guards attack near the basket late, so the final minutes will show whether Coach Harris trusts Reed in that group.
answerFacts: Harbor City's late-game lineups have been unsettled without Reed.
Denver's guards attack near the basket late in games.
The final minutes will show whether Coach Harris trusts Reed in the late-game group.

turns.coworkerLine: The article is not just 'Reed is back.' It is whether his return changes who plays the final minutes, and what Coach Harris or Reed can say on the record.
answerFacts: The article should not stop at Malik Reed being back.
The reporting question is whether Reed's return changes who plays the final minutes.
Coach Harris and Malik Reed can provide on-record attribution for role and health claims.

assignment_brief.title: 4:22 PM Assignment Desk
assignment_brief.content: You are at the arena media table before warmups, tipoff still ahead and Tessa's assignment thread already open. First, confirm what the newsroom wants, pull only the verified pregame materials, and decide what you need to watch when Reed steps on the floor.

slackMessages.content: Tonight's working question is whether Reed's return changes the closing group. Watch warmups, ask Coach Harris about late-game logic, and keep the story tied to what we can verify in the building.
Also: treat social media rumor chatter as unconfirmed unless a team, agent, league, or on-record source gives us something real. Do not make a social rumor the frame.

emails.subject: Official injury report - Harbor City vs. Denver
emails.content:
Official injury report, 4:05 PM ET update

Malik Reed - Available (returning from right ankle sprain)
Minutes restriction - None listed

Use the official status only as listed. Further health or readiness claims require direct attribution.

emails.subject: Arena media schedule - Cyclones vs. Denver
emails.content:
4:52 PM - Coach Len Harris pregame availability, interview room riser
7:10 PM - Tipoff
Postgame - Baseline scrum opens after locker-room cooling period
Postgame note - Malik Reed is expected at the baseline scrum if he closes or scores 20+

metrics.target: Same core group in late possessions
metrics.actual: Four different closing groups in the last five close games
Denver's lead guards average 8.7 fourth-quarter paint touches

socialPosts.content: Hearing Reed's return could be connected to bigger guard-market talks. If he closes tonight, watch the front office angle.
No official word, but the timeline is interesting: Reed back, scouts in the building, deadline chatter everywhere.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Health language
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not write that Reed is fully healed, medically cleared, or back to normal unless Reed, team PR, or another on-record source says it.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Evidence buckets
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Keep official status, your own observation, direct quotes, and unverified chatter separate in the draft.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Attribution bar
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Attribute every health or role claim: team PR listed, Coach Harris said, Reed said, or reporter observation.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Hold unverified material
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not publish social media rumors or hallway chatter unless an on-record source confirms them.

assignment_brief.imageBrief: Arena media room laptop with Slack assignment messages, official injury report, arena media schedule, stats dashboard, social media feed, CMS sidebar, deadline timer, and live game credential. The basketball court is visible through an open tunnel.

lead_angle.title: Reply With Your Reporting Angle
lead_angle.content: Review the tabs for the information currently available about tonight's game. You have 25 minutes before pregame coach availability. Reply to Tessa in #nba-cyclones with the angle you will pursue first.
lead_angle.prompt: Choose the reply you would send Tessa.

slackMessages.content: What is your working angle before coach availability? Need the one you can actually verify tonight.

Use the official status only as listed. Further health or readiness claims require direct attribution.

lead_angle.imageBrief: Reporter notebook with three circled possible angles: injury return, closing lineup, trade rumor. A phone shows social posts while an arena clock counts down.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Choose and send the most reportable angle to pursue from a finite editor prompt.
continuity: The selected reply appears in the redirect scene and sets the reporting plan frame.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The scene requires the player to transform visible reporting inputs into a beat-reporter artifact instead of answering from memory.

options.label: I will pursue whether Reed's return changes the late-game lineup. I can verify it with PR status, warmup observation, Coach Harris, and Reed postgame.
I will pursue the trade-rumor angle because it is moving on fan accounts and will get attention.
I will pursue a broad matchup-preview angle so the coverage stays safe.
I will pursue a Coach Harris mood angle because he seems tense and that could add color.

redirect_strong_angle.title: Angle Greenlit
redirect_strong_angle.content: Tessa likes the frame because it can be reported through official status, visible observation, coach availability, and Reed attribution. Now turn the angle into a reporting plan.

slackMessages.content: What is your working angle before coach availability? Need the one you can actually verify tonight.
Good. Build the plan around what you can verify: official status, what you personally observe, what Coach Harris can explain, and what Reed can say on the record.

redirect_strong_angle.imageBrief: Slack thread in an NBA arena media room where the editor greenlights a focused Reed-return and closing-lineup angle.

redirect_rumor.title: Rumor Without Grounding
redirect_rumor.content: The rumor gets attention, but nobody with direct knowledge will touch it on the record. Tessa redirects you to protect credibility and report what you can actually prove tonight.

slackMessages.content: What is your working angle before coach availability? Need the one you can actually verify tonight.
Monitor it, but do not make it your main angle. If it is not on record or confirmed, it stays out. Pivot back to Reed usage and the final-minutes lineup.

redirect_rumor.imageBrief: Phone screen full of rumor posts beside a reporter notebook marked no confirmation. Editor Slack message says verify before amplifying.

redirect_generic.title: Too Broad for Deadline
redirect_generic.content: A broad preview would be accurate but forgettable. Tessa needs you to spend limited access on a sharper question that can survive deadline editing.

slackMessages.content: What is your working angle before coach availability? Need the one you can actually verify tonight.
That is too wide for tonight. Narrow it: what does Reed return change late, and what can you verify before filing?

redirect_generic.imageBrief: A generic game preview draft fading behind a sharper sticky note: Reed return plus closing lineup.

redirect_color.title: Color Needs a Point
redirect_color.content: Coach mood may make the scene vivid, but mood is not the story by itself. Use color only when it supports a verified decision or consequence.

slackMessages.content: What is your working angle before coach availability? Need the one you can actually verify tonight.
Color is support, not the frame. Pursue the Reed/closing question first, then use scene detail if it helps the reader.

redirect_color.imageBrief: Coach at podium in a tense pregame media room, with reporter notes connecting mood to lineup decisions rather than gossip.

reporting_plan.title: Pregame Reporting Plan
reporting_plan.content: Before you enter coach availability, turn the angle into four concrete prep items: one question for Coach Harris, one question for Reed, one fact already confirmed by the given sources, and one unconfirmed claim you will leave out. The goal is not to write the article yet; it is to prepare what you will ask, what you can already say, and what you should not publish.
reporting_plan.prompt: Complete each required row for Reed's ankle-sprain return and the final-minutes lineup question. Use the tabs in this document window. Coach and Reed rows need a topic, an exact question, and why it matters; the confirmed-fact row needs a given source; the unconfirmed-claim row needs only what to avoid and why.

slackMessages.content: Tonight's working question is whether Malik Reed's return from a right ankle sprain changes who Coach Harris trusts in the final minutes. Watch warmups, ask Coach Harris about late-game logic, and keep the story tied to what we can verify in the building.
Also: treat social media rumor chatter as unconfirmed unless a team, agent, league, or on-record source gives us something real. Do not make a social media rumor the frame.
For the plan, cover four things: what you will ask Coach Harris, what you will ask Reed postgame, one fact already confirmed by the given sources, and one unconfirmed claim you will leave out.

continuity: The plan appears in coach_availability through prepNoteKey and sets up later Reed questions.

rowGuides.title: Coach availability question
rowGuides.sourceHint: Use the editor thread, PR status, media schedule, and late-game stats to write one question for Coach Harris.

need.label: Topic to ask about
need.placeholder: Example: Reed's role in the final minutes.

question.label: Exact question for Coach Harris
question.placeholder: Write one neutral, answerable question for Coach Harris.

risk.label: Why this matters
risk.placeholder: Explain what this answer helps you report.

rowGuides.title: Reed postgame question
rowGuides.sourceHint: Use the injury-return frame and postgame scrum access to write one question Reed can answer personally.

need.label: Topic to ask about
need.placeholder: Example: How Reed's ankle felt during late-game actions.

question.label: Exact question for Reed
question.placeholder: Write one neutral, answerable question for Reed.

risk.label: Why this matters
risk.placeholder: Explain what this answer helps you report.

rowGuides.title: Confirmed fact from the given sources
rowGuides.sourceHint: Use the PR email, arena schedule, game materials, or CMS rules. This row should be a fact already supported by those given sources, not a future interview question.

need.label: Fact you can safely use
need.placeholder: State one fact the given sources already support.

source.label: Given source that confirms it
source.placeholder: Example: Team PR injury report or Game Materials.

question.label: How it supports your angle
question.placeholder: Explain how this confirmed fact helps the Reed/lineup story.

need.label: Claim or rumor to avoid
need.placeholder: Name the unconfirmed claim you will leave out.

risk.label: Why it is not reportable yet

warmup_observation.title: Warmup Observation at Media Row
warmup_observation.content: You are at media row before tipoff. Open the observation popups in any order and keep one running note as you collect useful details about Reed. If something you observe gives you a question for Reed or Coach Harris, write that question in the same note so you can ask it later.
warmup_observation.simulatedAction: Inspect in-arena source surfaces and build one running observation note before interviews.
warmup_observation.learningGoal: Separate official status, personal observation, routine access detail, and unverified rumor while physically working from the arena.

physicalWorkDesign.realWorkflowScript: Arrive at media row, use the credential/deadline context, inspect the team PR status, Reed movement, closing-group drill, trainer contact, and rumor feed in any order, and keep one running observation note with clear attribution limits and later interview questions.

procedure: Add useful details to the same running note
Capture Reed or Coach Harris questions that the observation creates
Save and close popups in any order
Keep clear what each detail can and cannot support
stateChanges: Inspected surfaces are marked complete and the observation note is stored for grading.

actionClassification.mappingRationale: The student reads a visible source surface before using it.
The real job requires observation to become notes, not a hidden click result.

physicalWorkDesign.mainSceneRole: One 16:9 arena/media-row stage with court and work table hotspots.
popupSceneRoles: Readable closeups for PR email, Reed movement, closing-group drill, trainer contact, and rumor feed.
physicalWorkDesign.statePersistence: Inspected surfaces and the running reporter notes remain in the action log stored under warmup_observation.
physicalWorkDesign.antiButtonAudit: There is no generic complete-observation button; the player must inspect source surfaces and use the shared notes field in the popups.

background.prompt: Arena media-row view before an NBA game at Harbor City Arena. Foreground has a reporter work table with credential, notebook, phone, and a laptop open to a team PR injury-status email; midground shows Malik Reed warming up near the three-point line; a trainer stands near the baseline; empty lower-bowl seats and court lights fill the background. Leave clear clickable zones for the laptop PR email, phone feed, Reed movement, and trainer check-in.

coordinateMap.label: team PR injury email on laptop

backgrounds.prompt: Arena media-row view before an NBA game at Harbor City Arena. Foreground has a reporter work table with credential, notebook, phone, and a laptop open to a team PR injury-status email; midground shows Malik Reed warming up near the three-point line; a trainer stands near the baseline; empty lower-bowl seats and court lights fill the background. Leave clear clickable zones for the laptop PR email, phone feed, Reed movement, and trainer check-in.

readableSurfaces.prompt: Readable closeup of a laptop email from team PR. Text should include: "Malik Reed - Available. No minutes limit listed." Keep it realistic, like an email open on a reporter laptop at media row, not a printed note.

Closeup from media row of Malik Reed doing a hard lateral cut during warmups. Show athletic movement and a neutral facial expression, with no answer captions, checkmarks, conclusion labels, or documentation text.

Closeup of Malik Reed briefly speaking with a trainer near the baseline during warmups. Show a routine check-in posture, phones and media nearby, with no answer captions, checkmarks, conclusion labels, or documentation text.

Readable phone closeup showing fan-account posts speculating about a Malik Reed trade. Make usernames look fictional and unofficial. Include visible uncertainty such as "hearing chatter" and "unconfirmed" but no verification badges.

readableSurfaces.label: Team PR injury email
readableSurfaces.readableText: The team PR email lists Malik Reed as available tonight and does not list a minutes limit. That is enough to say what the team officially listed, but not enough to say how Reed feels or whether Coach Harris will close with him.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Add the official team status to your running note so you can attribute availability accurately. This can also set up a Coach Harris question about what will determine whether Reed closes.

readableSurfaces.label: Reed movement in warmups
readableSurfaces.readableText: From media row, Reed goes through lateral slides, a hard right-to-left cut, and corner threes. The hard cut is useful because you can later ask Reed what he felt on that specific movement instead of asking a vague injury question.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: You are writing one running note for all warmup observations. Add useful details and any Reed or Coach Harris question the observation gives you.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Add concise notes about what you observed. Keep useful details from earlier observations in this same note.
readableSurfaces.expectedObservation: A strong note says Reed moved through hard lateral cuts during warmups and turns that into a later Reed question about what he felt on the first hard cut, while treating the movement as reporter observation rather than medical clearance.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Observe movement, then write the note and the Reed question it creates.

readableSurfaces.label: Closing-group drill
readableSurfaces.readableText: During a late-clock defensive drill, Reed takes reps with the four players who have been projected as Harbor City's late-game group. Coach Harris stops one action, points Reed toward the weak-side switch, and restarts the possession.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Add the lineup signal to your note. This is a Coach Harris question: ask whether Reed's drill reps reflect a possible closing group or just warmup structure.

readableSurfaces.label: Trainer check-in
readableSurfaces.readableText: After the lateral drill, Reed walks to the baseline for a brief trainer check-in, points at the outside of his right shoe, and then returns to the warmup line. No one announces a status change while you are watching.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Add this only as observed context. It can become a Reed question about routine check-ins or equipment feel, but it is not a confirmed medical update.

readableSurfaces.label: Fan rumor feed
readableSurfaces.readableText: A fan account claims it is "hearing chatter" about Reed's future and labels the post "unconfirmed." It is not a team, league, agent, or player source.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Inspect the rumor provenance before deciding whether it belongs in copy.

successConditions: Includes official team status in the running note set.
Turns Reed movement into a specific later Reed question.
Turns closing-group drill reps into a specific later Coach Harris question.
Recognizes trainer contact as observed context, not confirmed medical information.
failureModes: Treats warmup observation as official medical clearance.
Publishes fan rumor or hallway chatter as fact.

coach_availability.title: Coach Harris Availability
coach_availability.content:
Coach Len Harris is at the interview-room riser before tipoff. Before you press Start conversation, review the support notes on the right, including your pregame reporting plan and warmup memo. Use those notes to prepare two concise questions. Do not turn what you hear into a permanent medical or rotation claim yet.

Take notes in the box while you interview him. You will write the deadline article from your own interview notes, transcripts, and the materials you collected in the workplace.
coach_availability.goalPrompt: You are Coach Len Harris of the fictional Harbor City Cyclones. Answer like a guarded but professional NBA coach in an in-person media availability. If the reporter asks about Reed status or closing lineup logic with concise, fair wording, say Reed is available and the staff will watch game flow, then give the usable quote: "Closing groups are situational. Tonight, if that group earns it, we will ride it." If the reporter asks rumor or personality questions, redirect to the game. After the reporter has asked two substantive questions or after four user turns, wrap up with a closing line and end the availability; do not ask follow-up questions.
coach_availability.meetingContext: The user is playing the reporter, an entry-level NBA beat reporter standing in the arena interview room before tipoff. They prepared a reporting plan and need a coach quote about Reed usage and the closing lineup.
coach_availability.playerGoal: In 2-4 turns, ask two concise pregame questions grounded in your support notes, without making a permanent medical or rotation claim.
coach_availability.preStartPrompt: Before starting, read the support notes on the right. Use your pregame reporting plan and warmup memo to prepare two concise questions.
coach_availability.notePrompt: Coach Harris notes for your article
coach_availability.noteHelper: Take notes during this availability. You will write the deadline article from your notes, so capture usable Coach Harris wording, clean paraphrases, and anything that still needs Reed or postgame confirmation.
coach_availability.notePlaceholder: Example: Coach Harris said closing groups are situational... / Need Reed later on what he felt closing...
coach_availability.prepReferenceContent: Facts available: Team PR lists Reed as available with no minutes limit. Story question: whether Reed return changes late-game usage. Risk: one pregame answer cannot prove a permanent rotation. Avoid the unverified trade rumor.
coach_availability.imageBrief: In-person NBA pregame interview room. Coach Len Harris stands at a small riser with team backdrop while reporters hold recorders and phones. First-person reporter perspective, no laptop or video call grid.
coach_availability.endpoint: End once you have one attributable coach answer and know what still needs Reed or postgame attribution.
coach_availability.successCriteria: You keep the question grounded in the available materials, avoid rumor language, and preserve the limits of a pregame answer.

inputArtifacts.label: Pregame reporting plan
inputArtifacts.summary: Student reporting needs and questions from the prior plan.

inputArtifacts.label: Warmup game memo
inputArtifacts.summary: Player-written running notes from the warmup observation scene.

inputArtifacts.label: Coach availability facts
inputArtifacts.summary: Reed official status, story question, and rumor/overclaiming risks.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Ask concise, attributable coach availability questions.
continuity: Coach answer informs possession watching, live note capture, and postgame questions.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Beat reporters routinely use short coach availability windows to clarify what can and cannot be reported before a game.

possession_timeline_watch.title: Possession Timeline Watch
possession_timeline_watch.content: You are back at media row for the closing stretch. Follow the game as it tightens, stops, restarts, and shifts around Reed's minutes. Write at least three useful notes for postgame; you do not need to fill every note box, but capture any moment that could help you ask sharper Reed questions than the box score alone would.
possession_timeline_watch.referenceTitle: What you know before the final stretch
possession_timeline_watch.referenceContent:
Pregame frame: Malik Reed is returning from an ankle sprain, and Coach Harris said closing groups are situational.

Reporting discipline:
- Note what you can personally observe.
- Mark what needs Reed or Coach Harris attribution before publishing.
- Do not treat rumor, body language, or a single possession as a confirmed medical or trade fact.
- Your notes will feed the postgame scrum and deadline story.
possession_timeline_watch.summaryPrompt: Based on the possessions you just watched, read the Reed question advice, then prepare two concise Reed postgame scrum questions to ask next.

questionReference.title: Pregame Reed question
questionReference.helper: You can reuse this question as one of your two questions, or revise it now based on what you saw late in the game.

possession_timeline_watch.questionGuidance:
Good Reed questions should help you get usable attribution for tonight's story.

Before writing each question:
- Anchor it in one specific late-game moment from your notes.
- Ask what Reed personally felt, noticed, or understood.
- Keep the wording neutral. Do not sound accusatory, leading, medical, or rumor-driven.
- If the answer belongs to Coach Harris, PR, or another source, do not make Reed confirm it.
questionPlaceholders: Ask Reed about what he personally felt or noticed on a specific late possession.
Ask Reed for a usable quote that connects the timeline to tonight's story.

categories.label: Confirmed fact
categories.description: A scoreboard, box-score, or visible play fact you can safely use.

categories.label: Ask Reed
categories.description: Something Reed can explain or attribute after the game.

categories.label: Ask coach
categories.description: A lineup, tactical, or rotation question for Coach Harris.

categories.label: Scene color
categories.description: Observed detail that adds texture but should not carry the story alone.

categories.label: Hold / verify
categories.description: Interesting but not publishable without a stronger source.

events.headline: Reed checks back in with the closing group
events.description: Coach Harris points Reed toward the scorer's table. Reed jogs in without an obvious limp, but he glances toward the trainer before the inbound.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: would you record the substitution, the trainer glance, both, or neither? Keep observation separate from medical conclusion.

events.headline: Denver forces Reed through a high screen
events.description: Denver targets Reed on the ball. Reed slides laterally, recovers over the screen, and contests without grabbing at the ankle.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is an eye-test moment. Record what you saw, then decide who needs to confirm how it felt.

events.headline: Denver's second unit finishes a 13-2 run
events.description: The Cyclones bench looks quiet during the timeout. Coach Harris keeps Reed with the closing group and sends the same five back out.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: does this belong as a bench-problem angle, closing-lineup context, or just background?

events.headline: Reed draws help and finds the corner
events.description: Reed turns the corner, pulls a second defender, and passes to the weak-side corner for a three. The arena shifts from anxious to loud.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is a usable basketball detail. Decide whether it is a confirmed fact, scene color, or a question for postgame.

events.headline: A fan-account DM pushes a trade rumor
events.description: During the stoppage, your phone buzzes with an unverified fan-account DM claiming another team contacted the Cyclones to ask whether Reed could be traded. No team, agent, league, or on-record source is named.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this may be noisy, not news. Decide whether it belongs anywhere in your working notes before the buzzer.

events.headline: Reed switches onto Denver's final high screen
events.description: Denver hunts a switch. Reed stays in front long enough to force a late pass, and the Cyclones secure the rebound that seals the 112-107 win.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is likely part of the article angle. Note the play cleanly and think about a specific Reed question.

events.headline: Final score board locks in the postgame facts
events.description: The in-arena result board shows Harbor City Cyclones 112, Denver Altitude 107. The official box score credits Malik Reed with 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is the verified final and Reed's official line. Decide how you would record the result and box score before walking into the scrum.

media_scrum.title: Malik Reed Postgame Scrum
media_scrum.content:
After the game, Malik Reed stops near the baseline for a short in-person scrum. You prepared two questions from the possession timeline. Ask those questions clearly.

Take notes in the box while you interview Reed. You will write the deadline article from your own interview notes, transcripts, and the materials you collected in the workplace.
media_scrum.goalPrompt: You are Malik Reed, veteran guard for the fictional Harbor City Cyclones. You are cooperative but tired of vague injury questions. If the reporter asks broad questions, answer with polished generalities about feeling good and helping the team. If they ask precise, fair questions, reveal these details across separate answers: the ankle felt stable on hard cuts, the training staff removed the minutes limit this morning, halftime trainer check-ins were routine, he expected to close if the defense held, and he does not want the night framed only as a personal comeback. If the question asks for Coach Harris's tactical reasoning or PR's official status, answer only your personal view and say Coach Harris or PR would own the final confirmation. Do not volunteer everything at once. After the reporter has asked two substantive questions or after four user turns, wrap up with a closing line and end the scrum; do not continue opening new topics.
media_scrum.meetingContext: The user is playing the reporter, an entry-level NBA beat reporter in an in-person postgame arena scrum. They prepared two Reed questions from the possession timeline and should ask them clearly.
media_scrum.playerGoal: Ask the two Reed questions you prepared from your possession notes.
media_scrum.notePrompt: Malik Reed notes for your article
media_scrum.noteHelper: Take notes during the scrum. The deadline article will rely on your Reed notes and transcript, so capture exact wording, what Reed can personally confirm, and anything that still needs Coach Harris, PR, or another source.
media_scrum.notePlaceholder: Example: Reed said... / Quote or paraphrase I can use... / Hold: needs Coach Harris or PR...
media_scrum.imageBrief: In-person NBA postgame media scrum near the baseline. Reporters hold phones and recorders toward veteran guard Malik Reed in team gear, with court lights, camera operators, and the tunnel behind him. No video call grid or laptop screen.
media_scrum.endpoint: End once you have asked your two prepared Reed questions and captured at least one useful Reed answer.
media_scrum.successCriteria: You ask both prepared Reed questions and take usable notes from Reed's answers.

continuity: Scrum transcript feeds the deadline story.

fast_gamer.title: Write the Game Article
fast_gamer.content: It is 10:34 PM. Tessa needs the top of the article now. Write from the Player Notes and Results tabs. Do not add quotes, medical conclusions, rotation claims, or rumor language that are not supported by those tabs.
fast_gamer.prompt: Write 120-220 words for the top of the game article based only on your Player Notes and Results tabs. Include the final score, Reed's ankle-sprain return, the final-minutes lineup angle, and one attributed quote or paraphrase only if your notes support it. Do not include unverified trade chatter.

editor_slack.title: Editor Slack Update
editor_slack.content: Tessa has read your game article draft and sends a Slack follow-up based on what you actually filed. Reply with one concise editor-ready answer about how you would revise the copy using only the reporting already in hand.
editor_slack.aiInitialMessagePrompt: Write Tessa's first Slack message after reading the submitted game article draft. Be a sharp, supportive NBA editor: name one specific thing the draft is doing well, name one concrete reporting/copy issue that follows from the actual words in the draft, and ask one focused follow-up the reporter can answer using only reporting already in hand. If you ask for a quote or attribution, include an if-not path, such as: if you did not get that quote, tell me what line you would cut, weaken, or reattribute. If the draft is cautious but light on news, ask how to add the official stat line or turn the safer frame into a stronger lead. Do not assign new reporting, do not ask them to go back to Reed/Coach Harris/PR, and do not reuse a predetermined angle menu. Keep it under 95 words.
editor_slack.aiInitialMessageFallback: I read the article draft. The frame is disciplined, but we need the copy sharpened from what you already have: what is the strongest confirmed news angle, and what line would you cut, weaken, or reattribute if you do not have the quote to support it?
editor_slack.goalPrompt: Evaluate the reporter's answer to your draft-specific Slack feedback. A strong response is concise, answers the exact follow-up you asked, explains how to revise the filed copy using only current reporting, names what is confirmed, and flags any claim that must be cut, weakened, or reattributed. If the reporter admits they did not get a quote or confirmation, reward the honesty and tell them exactly how to revise the copy without that material. After one player reply, respond with a brief, useful editor acknowledgement and one copy-edit direction. Do not assign new reporting, do not tell them to ask Reed, Coach Harris, PR, or another source later, do not ask another question, and do not literally say 'thread closed.'
editor_slack.playerGoal: Reply to Tessa's draft-specific feedback with a concise copy-edit answer using only reporting already in hand.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: Slack thread with editor prompt and source continuity

final_story.prompt: Write the final filed version of the story. Cut, soften, or reattribute anything you cannot verify.

malik.name: Malik Reed
malik.persona: Malik is a respected veteran guard returning from an ankle sprain. He is media-savvy, careful with medical details, and more open when a reporter asks precise, fair questions about the game instead of pushing him toward a dramatic comeback narrative.

coach.name: Len Harris
coach.persona: Len Harris is a veteran NBA head coach who protects competitive details but respects reporters who ask concise, fair, basketball-specific questions. He avoids rumor, dislikes personality framing, and gives usable quotes when the question is tied to lineup logic or game context.

tessa.name: Tessa Ortiz
tessa.role: Senior NBA Editor, Full Court Wire
tessa.persona: Tessa is a sharp national NBA editor who cares about speed, attribution, and clean story framing. She likes young reporters who are direct about what they know, honest about what they do not know, and disciplined enough to leave unverified material out of copy.

intro.welcomeBody: Step into an entry-level NBA beat reporter's game night: frame the story, observe from the arena, ask questions in person, sort messy notes, write fast, and protect accuracy under deadline pressure.

steps.label: Frame
steps.content: Choose a reportable angle that fits the game, the audience, and what you can verify.

steps.label: Observe
steps.content: Use the arena setting to record what you can see while separating observation from official status.

steps.label: Ask
steps.content: Use limited coach and player access to ask precise questions that produce usable attribution.

steps.label: File
steps.content: Write and communicate clearly enough for an editor to publish on deadline.

glossary.beat: A recurring reporting assignment focused on one team, league, institution, or topic. A beat reporter develops context, sources, and pattern recognition over time.
glossary.gamer: A same-day game story or recap, usually filed quickly after the final buzzer and updated with postgame quotes or analysis.
glossary.nut graf: The paragraph that explains the central point, context, or significance of a story after the lead.
glossary.scrum: A group interview setting where several reporters ask questions around a player or coach, often with limited time.
glossary.availability: A scheduled period when players or coaches are available to reporters before or after practice, shootaround, or games.
glossary.on the record: Information that can be quoted or attributed publicly according to the source identity and terms.
glossary.attribution: Language that tells readers where information came from, such as a player quote, team announcement, public record, or reporter observation.
glossary.color: Observed scene detail that adds texture to a story, such as body language, arena atmosphere, or interactions, without replacing verified reporting.
glossary.copy desk: Editors who review stories for accuracy, grammar, style, clarity, headlines, and publication readiness.
glossary.minutes limit: A restriction on how much a player is expected or allowed to play, often after injury or during return-to-play management.

sample_answers.pregame_plan: [{"rowTitle":"Coach availability question","need":"Late-game trust and closing-lineup logic with Reed available.","question":"With Malik listed available and no minutes limit listed, what will determine whether he can close tonight?","risk":"This answer explains the basketball decision without guessing about Reed's health or a permanent rotation change."},{"rowTitle":"Reed postgame question","need":"How Reed felt when the ankle had to respond at game speed.","question":"What was the first play tonight when the ankle had to respond to a hard cut or defensive slide?","risk":"This gives Reed's own account instead of turning observation into a health claim."},{"rowTitle":"Confirmed fact from the given sources","need":"Reed is listed available after a right ankle sprain, with no minutes restriction listed.","source":"Team PR official injury report","question":"This supports a Reed-return angle while limiting the copy to official availability, not full health."},{"rowTitle":"Unconfirmed claim to leave out","need":"Social media trade-rumor chatter about Reed.","risk":"It is not reportable because there is no team, agent, league, or on-record confirmation."}]
sample_answers.warmup_observation: Team PR lists Reed available with no minutes limit. From media row, Reed went through lateral slides, a hard right-to-left cut, and corner threes; ask Reed later what he felt on that first hard cut rather than turning the observation into a health claim. Reed also took late-clock defensive drill reps with the projected closing group; ask Coach Harris whether that reflects a possible closing group or just warmup structure. Trainer check-in after the lateral drill is observed context only, not a status update. Fan-account rumor is unconfirmed and should stay out unless verified.
sample_answers.possession_timeline_summary: 1) Ask Reed how the ankle felt when Denver forced him through the high screen and on the final switch. 2) Ask how he read his role in the closing group after the bench run and corner assist.
sample_answers.live_game_note_rationale: The final-stretch note creates new postgame scrum questions because Reed playing the final 6:12 makes the closing lineup the game angle. The fan-account DM stays out because it has no named or on-record source.
sample_answers.fast_gamer: HARBOR CITY - Malik Reed's return did not solve every Cyclones problem, but it gave Harbor City the closing stability it had been missing. Reed scored 24 points and played the final 6:12 of Wednesday's 112-107 win over the Denver Altitude, his first game back after an ankle sprain that had kept the team's late-game rotation unsettled. The team did not list Reed on a minutes limit, and Reed said afterward that the ankle felt stable when he had to cut hard. Coach Len Harris stopped short of declaring a permanent closing group, calling the lineup situational, but the choice to leave Reed on the floor through Denver's final push made the night's clearest statement.
sample_answers.editor_slack: I would revise the copy around the closing-stretch usage, not a broad ankle claim. Confirmed: Reed scored 24, had 6 assists and 2 steals, team PR listed him available with no minutes restriction, and Coach Harris said closing groups are situational. If I do not have a Reed ankle quote in my notes, I would cut the line implying Reed commented on the ankle and attribute only availability to team PR.
sample_answers.final_story:
Headline: Reed return steadies Cyclones late in 112-107 win

HARBOR CITY - Malik Reed's first game back from an ankle sprain gave Harbor City more than a scoring lift. Reed finished with 24 points, 6 assists and 2 steals, but the clearer story was that the Cyclones trusted him through the final 6:12 of a 112-107 win over Denver.

Team PR had Reed listed as available without a minutes restriction, but the copy should stop short of saying the ankle is fully back unless Reed said that directly. The supported frame is that Harbor City had Reed available late and used him in decisive possessions, including the corner-three setup after Denver cut the lead to 103-101. Coach Len Harris said closing groups are situational, which keeps the lineup question open rather than turning one finish into a permanent rotation claim.
sample_answers.coach_availability: Ask: Coach, with Malik available and no minutes limit listed, what will determine whether he can close tonight? Ask: What do you need from that closing group defensively against Denver? Follow-up: Is this a matchup decision or something you are evaluating longer term?
sample_answers.media_scrum: Ask: Malik, what was the first play tonight when you felt the ankle had to respond at game speed? Ask: Did the staff give you any cap or check-in point before you could close? Ask: When Coach went back to you late, what did that group give you defensively? Ask: Is there anything about the ankle you still need to manage tomorrow?

criteria.name: 記事角度の選択
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 噂ではなく、ニュース価値、検証可能性、締切、読者への意味を見て取材の角度を選べている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 取材計画
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: この計画タスクだけで、Coach Harrisへの質問、Reed本人への質問、与えられたソースで既に確認済みの事実、記事に入れない未確認情報を4行で具体化できている。Coach/Reedの行では話題、正確な質問、なぜ重要かが分かり、確認済み事実の行では根拠となるgiven sourceが明確で、未確認情報の行ではなぜまだ報じられないかが説明されている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 練習観察
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 公式情報、観察事実、噂、医学的断定を分けて記録し、公開できる情報と保留すべき情報を区別できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 安全上の必須条件を飛ばした場合は、他の要素ができていても満点にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 監督への質問
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 実際の取材で、監督に聞くべき範囲の質問を短く具体的に行い、本人にしか答えにくい内容を混ぜすぎない。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 選手囲み取材
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 実際の囲みで、選手に答えやすく、記事に使える発言を引き出す質問をし、圧迫や誘導を避けられている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 試合中観察
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 試合中の重要な流れ、観察、数字、使える場面を、速報や最終記事に使える形で整理できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 速報原稿
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 確認済み事実と取材内容だけで、締切に間に合うリード、要点、帰属のある速報原稿を書けている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 編集者連絡
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 編集者の指摘に対して、何を直すか、何を未確認として避けるか、現在使える根拠を短く返せている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 最終記事
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 最終記事だけを根拠に、見出し、構成、根拠、引用、未確認情報の扱いが整った記事にできている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。
